scenarios.query: I ran three lenses on a PR. reviewing-performance-and-efficiency says the new Redis cache at orders/pricing.py:42 has no invalidation and no profile justifies it. checking-restraint also flags orders/pricing.py:42 saying no profile justifies the cache, and separately flags a retry count made configurable for one caller. hunting-silent-failures says the retry loop at orders/payment.py:88 swallows the provider error after the last attempt. Merge these into one review.
expected_behavior: Deduplicates the 'no profile justifies the cache' point raised by both performance and restraint at orders/pricing.py:42 into a single finding rather than reporting it twice
Ranks the swallowed-error finding above the cache and config nits, and ends on a single verdict (approve with changes / block)
Surfaces the restraint-vs-performance tension on the cache and resolves it with the default (no optimization without a profile) instead of silently dropping one side

scenarios.query: Two lenses disagree about the same code. reviewing-module-design says extracting a PricingStrategy interface clarifies the seam. checking-restraint says it's a single implementation with no second consumer, so it's premature abstraction. Both point at pricing/strategy.py. Give me one merged conclusion.
expected_behavior: Does not report both as independent findings with opposite conclusions; reconciles them into one resolution
Applies the restraint-vs-module-design default: prefer the simpler design until a second concrete consumer exists
States the trade-off so the author could override it with evidence (a second strategy appearing)

scenarios.query: All four lenses I ran on a one-line typo fix in the README each reported 'No findings'. What's the synthesized review?
expected_behavior: Produces a 'No findings' / approve report and adds no invented findings or speculative suggestions
Does not manufacture a harsher verdict (no 'approve with changes' or 'block') when nothing was flagged
Stays short rather than padding the report to look thorough

scenarios.query: I have a stack of findings from five lenses on one PR: one is a SQL injection on an untrusted query param, two are duplicate notes about the same missing null check at the same line, a couple are naming nits, and one is a formatting comment. How should the final report be organized and what verdict?
expected_behavior: Floats the SQL injection to the top as the highest-severity (Blocker) finding regardless of which lens raised it, and blocks on it
Collapses the two duplicate null-check notes into a single finding attributed to one (primary) owner
Groups remaining findings by severity with nits last, keeping each finding to a line rather than restating full lens detail

scenarios.query: Findings from a checkout-flow PR: tracing-correctness flags an off-by-one at checkout/cart.py:60 that drops the last line item (a real bug). A reviewer also notes the error shown to users on a declined card just says 'Error 4012' with no guidance — a confusing, product-owned UX call, not an engineering one. And the cancel button sits below the fold on mobile, a design concern. Merge these into one review and tell me what blocks.
expected_behavior: Blocks (or approve-with-changes) on the off-by-one as a defect that engineering owns and sets the verdict
Surfaces the 'Error 4012' message and the below-the-fold cancel button under a Routed section tagged route: product and route: design rather than silently dropping them as out of scope

Does not let the product/design routed findings set the engineering block/approve verdict by themselves, and does not adjudicate the product/UX calls itself — it routes the decision to the right owner

scenarios.query: A PR adds a feature correctly. The team has opted into improvement suggestions. Lenses report: no defects; checking-restraint notes a now-dead helper at utils/legacy.py:12 left after the refactor; reviewing-naming suggests renaming `d` to `delay` at worker.py:30; and one lens suggests reordering two independent methods into a different but equivalent arrangement 'for cohesion'. Synthesize the review.
expected_behavior: Reports the dead-code removal and the `d`->`delay` rename as improvement-valence, nit-severity, route: implementer suggestions (apply/defer/ignore) under an Improvements section, and ends on approve (no defects means it does not block or force changes)
Drops the method-reorder suggestion because it offers a merely equivalent arrangement, not a genuine improvement (the anti-churn / value bar), rather than surfacing change-for-change's-sake
Keeps improvements clearly separated from defects and does not inflate the verdict on account of the optional nits
